The effects of fuel processing by the ion-exchange method at a rate of once per year and at a rate of 12 times per year were compared for the Interim Design Reactor fueled with U $^{233}$ .

The nuclear performance of a one-region, graphite-moderated, unreflected, thorium-conversion, molten-salt-fueled reactor was studied to evaluate the effects of processing the fuel at the rate of once per year and 12 times per year, the effects of decreasing the thickness of the core vessel and adding a blanket, and the effects of using a graphite core vessel and a blanket.

The results of the calculations indicated that the nuclear performance of the one-region reactor fueled with $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ and processed by either the volatility or ion-exchange methods at the rate of one fuel volume per year is competitive with the best performance of the solid fuel systems, and therefore the molten-salt reactor will yield lower overall fuel costs when the potential economies of continuous, chemical processing of the fluid fuel are realized.
It was found that with 13 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ in the fuel, a maximum regeneration ratio of 0.846 could be obtained with an inventory of approximately $1150\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ .
With 7 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ in the fuel, an estimated maximum regeneration ratio of 0.798 at an inventory of approximately $900\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ was obtained.

Results were obtained for reactors with power levels of 125 and 250 Mw.
It was found that the only reactor with a doubling time less than 20 years was the 250-Mw reactor having a fuel volume fraction of 0.10.
Work is continuing on the design of a 30-Mw, one-region, experimental, molten-salt reactor.
The modified Fulton-Sylphon bellows-mounted seal being subjected to an endurance test in a PK-P type of centrifugal pump has continued to seal satisfactorily for more than 12,000 hr of operation.

Operation of an MF type of centrifugal pump with fuel 30 as the circulated fluid has continued to be satisfactory through more than 15,500 hr, with the past 13,500 hr of operation being under cavitation damage conditions.
A small frozen-lead pump seal on a 3/16-in.-dia shaft has operated since the first 100 hr of 7500 hr of operation with no lead leakage.
A similar seal on a 3 1/4-in.-dia shaft has operated 3600 hr, with an average leakage rate of $9\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{hr}$ .
The operation of long-term forced-circulation corrosion-testing loops continued in 15 test stands.
The viscosity showed some increase (of the order of $10\%$ ) as the percentage of $\mathrm{UF}_4$ and $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ in the mixtures was increased.
Corrosion studies were completed on four INOR-3 and four Inconel thermal-convection loops.
Metallographic examination of the INOR-3 loops, three of which operated for 1000 hr and the other for one year, revealed no observable attack.
The Inconel loops, which represented three one-year tests and one 1000-hr test, showed intergranular void attack to depths ranging from 4 to 15 mils.

In static pressure penetration tests at 150 psia and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , partially degassed graphite was penetrated throughout by fuel 30 ( $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ ) but was not penetrated by fuel 130 ( $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - UF_4}$ ), as indicated by macroscopic examination.
Thermal cycling of the graphite that was penetrated by fuel 30 did not result in damage to the graphite.
A series of tests was run to further investigate the precipitation of uranium from fuel 130 held in a graphite crucible at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Several brazing alloys were tested for compatibility with fuel 130.
52 (89% Ni-4% B-5% Si-2% Fe) and pure copper brazed to Inconel and INOR-8 showed good resistance to fuel 130 in 1000-hr tests at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a thermal-convection loop.
The following brazing alloys showed some attack or porosity as a result of similar exposure to fuel 130: General Electric alloy No.
31 (70% Ni-19% Cr-11% Si), Coast Metals alloy No.
53 (81% Ni-8% Cr-4% Si-4% B-3% Fe), and a gold-nickel alloy (82% Au-18% Ni).

A specimen of INOR-8 was examined for evidence of carburization that had been exposed to fuel 130 for $4000\mathrm{hr}$ in a thermal-convection loop hot leg at a temperature of $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The presence of a 10-in. graphite insert in the hot leg of this loop did not cause carburization of the INOR-8.
No new creep data for INOR-8 became available during the quarter.
Creep tests are presently in progress at 1100 and $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the fluoride salts of interest as the test environments, and these tests are expected to run in excess of 10,000 hr.
The critical results obtained from rotating-beam fatigue tests at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ indicate that INOR-8 has significantly better fatigue resistance than Inconel.
Creep and relaxation tests indicate that an unstable condition exists in the temperature range from 1100 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Based on the results of tensile tests conducted on specimens of INOR-8 aged for 10,000 hr in the temperature range of 1000 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , it has been concluded that INOR-8 does not exhibit embrittling tendencies that can be attributed to high-temperature instability.
Porous nickel tubes have been ordered from Micro Metallic Corp. to determine the feasibility of cladding the material with Inconel and INOR-8.

In the study of the chemistry of the corrosion process, further samples of melts from operating INOR-8 and Inconel forced-circulation loops were analyzed for chromium.
The chromium concentration in the $\mathrm{LiF - BeF}_2\mathrm{-ThF}_4\mathrm{-UF}_4$ mixture in the INOR-8 loop reached a plateau of about 550 ppm after about 1200 hr of operation.
The chromium concentration of the same salt mixture in the Inconel loop increased more rapidly than in the INOR-8 loop and after 1700 hr was still increasing.

Further measurements of the solubility of neptunium (IV) in aqueous solutions saturated with $\mathrm{LiF - BeF}_2\mathrm{-ThF}_4\mathrm{-UF}_4$ indicate that a solubility of the order of 0.0002 to 0.00005 mole % may be expected in actual processing.

It was reported previously<sup>1</sup> that substantial savings in fuel burnup and inventory could be achieved in the Interim Design Reactor<sup>2</sup> fueled with $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ by passing the fuel salt rapidly through beds of $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ to remove the rare earth fission products.
The effect of ion exchange processing on the performance of the same reactor system fueled with $\mathsf{U}^{233}$ has now been studied.
The results for two different processing rates - once per year and 12 times per year - are compared in Fig.
Processing at the rate of once per year (1.7 ft<sup>3</sup>/day) could be performed either by the fluoride-volatility method<sup>1</sup> or by the ion-exchange method.
It is doubtful therefore that the ion-exchange process offers any substantial economic advantage in the Interim Design Reactor system fueled with U $^{233}$ .
The nuclear performance without processing to remove fission products of a reactor having a spherical core $14$ ft in diameter and fuel channels 3.6 in.
ID arranged in a square lattice on 8 in. centers, was described previously. $^{3}$ The performance of the same system with various processing rates and with other modifications has now been
studied, and typical results are presented in Table 1.1.1 and in Fig.
1.1.2, where regeneration ratio, critical inventory, and net cumulative burnup are plotted as functions of time of operation.
Without processing, the regeneration ratio falls from 0.8 to 0.5 in 20 years.

The fuel additions required to override fission-product poisons are large; the critical inventory rises from 300 to $1800\mathrm{kg}$ of fuel, giving an average specific power of only $0.4\mathrm{Mw/kg}$ .
The burnup is also large, averaging $0.45\mathrm{g/Mwd}$ .
Processing the fuel salt to remove fission products at the rate of one fuel volume per year (2.5 ft $^3$ /day) stabilizes the regeneration ratio at above 0.8.
The inventory averages about 750 kg.
The burn-up is reduced to 0.21 g/Mwd.
Increasing the processing rate to 12 fuel volumes per year (30 ft $^3$ /day) improves the performance only slightly.

Accordingly, the thickness of the INOR-8 reactor vessel wall was reduced to $1/2$ in. for the calculations, and a 30-in. blanket containing 13 mole $\% \mathrm{ThF}_4$ in a mixture of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ was added.
The blanket system was processed 12 times a year by the fluoride-volatility method to remove $\mathrm{U}^{233}$ , which was added to the fuel salt.
The fuel was processed 12 times a year by the ion-exchange method to remove fission products.
The results of the calculations are presented in Table 1.l.2, and the performance is indicated in Fig.
1.l.2 by the lines labeled $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ 12,B.
The regeneration ratio averaged in excess of 0.96 and approached 1.0 in the steady state.
The critical inventory was not decreased significantly, but was shifted toward $\mathrm{U}^{233}$ in composition (90%).
The burnup was reduced to 0.056 g/Mwd, and the reactor was practically self-sustaining.

In a further extension of the calculations, the nuclear benefits to be obtained by the use of a graphite core vessel (in place of INOR-8) and fueling with $\mathbf{U}^{233}$ were studied.
The results of the calculations are presented in Table 1.1.2, and the performance is indicated in Fig.
1.1.2 by the lines labeled U<sup>233</sup> 12,B.
The regeneration ratio is stabilized above 1.06, the average inventory is about 650 kg, and the cumulative net burnup is slightly negative, amounting to -250 kg of U<sup>233</sup> in 20 years.
However, 76 kg of this is required as increased inventory in the fuel salt to override fission products and nonfissionable isotopes.

It is concluded that the nuclear performance of the one-region reactor fueled with $\mathbf{U}^{235}$ and processed by either the volatility or ion-exchange methods at the rate of one fuel volume per year is competitive with the best performance of the solid fuel systems, and therefore the molten-salt reactor will yield lower over-all fuel costs when the potential economies of continuous, chemical processing of the fluid fuel are realized.
The reactor considered in the study consists of a cylindrical core, 15 ft in dia and 15 ft high, surrounded by a 2.5-ft-thick graphite reflector contained in an INOR-8 pressure shell 1.5 in. thick.
The core is penetrated by cylindrical fuel passages arranged in an 8-in. triangular lattice parallel to the core axis.
The resulting unit cells are hexagonal and 15 ft long.

Design of 30-Mw Experimental Reactor
Work is continuing on the 30-Mw, one-region, experimental, molten-salt reactor described in the previous report.
The fuel drain system consists of four 2-1/2-ft-dia vessels approximately 20 ft long.
A multiplication constant of less than 0.5 was obtained for one drain vessel presumed to contain the entire fuel system $\mathrm{UF}_4$ inventory in an LiF-BeF $_2$ salt mixture.

As a further check, the $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ was assumed to settle out in the vessel as an oxide compound, and multiplication constants are being obtained for four different geometries of fuel concentrate, with one edge reflected by the salt mixture.
A maximum multiplication constant has not yet been obtained, but, with the fuel concentrated in a cube, the multiplication constant was 0.83.

The sixth test of a journal bearing operating in molten-salt fuel 130 (LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ , 62-37-1 mole %), as described previously, was terminated on schedule.
During this test the temperature of the salt was increased in $50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ increments from 1200 to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Performance was satisfactory throughout the entire test, which was conducted at a constant journal speed of 1200 rpm and a constant radial load of 200 lb.
The total operating time for this test was 192 hr.
This test also consisted of steady-state operation at a journal speed of 1200 rpm and a radial load of 200 lb, but start-stop operations were carried out three times at each test temperature.
The temperature was varied as in the sixth test from 1200 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in $50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ increments.
On attempting a second restart at the $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature level, an increase in the power required was noted, and the test was halted.
The total operating time for this test was 216 hr.
The eighth test was conducted to study the effect of increasing the bearing radial clearance (measured at room temperature) to 0.007 in.
All bearings previously tested had a radial clearance of 0.005 in.
Molten-salt fuel 130 at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was used for this test, and the bearing radial load was varied from 50 to 500 lb at each of two journal speeds, 600 and 1200 rpm.
After 356 hr of operation, including 39 start-stop sequences, a sudden increase in power halted the test.
At the time the power increase occurred the journal was operating at 600 rpm, and the load was being increased from 300 to 400 lb; the load had reached 382 lb.
A carburized INOR-8 journal was used with an uncarburized INOR-8 bearing for the ninth test.
The tester was started and stopped 260 times during a period of operation of 272 hr at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The modified Fulton-Sylphon bellows-mounted seal² being subjected to an endurance test in a PK-P type of centrifugal pump has accumulated an additional 1848 hr of operation since the previous report period, for a total of 12,478 hr.
The pump has been operating at a temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a shaft speed of 2500 rpm, and a NaK flow rate of 1200 gpm.
The maximum test-seal leakage rate was 3.5 cm³/day; however, on an averaged basis, the leakage is still negligible.
An MF type of centrifugal pump has continued in operation, $^{3}$ and has logged more than 15,500 hr (approximately 1 3/4 yr) of continuous operation.

During the last 13,465 hr, a cavitation endurance test has been under way with the pump operating at the steady-state conditions of 2700 rpm, 645 gpm, and 2.5-psig pump tank cover gas pressure at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
One stop was of 65 min duration for replacing brushes in the motor-generator set.
One stop was of 30 min duration for calibrating the pressure-measuring devices.
One stop was for 60 min for freeing the system throttle valve that had become stuck.
The small frozen-lead pump seal being tested on a 3/16-in.-dia shaft, as described previously, has operated continuously since it was
started on June 13, 1958.
The accumulated operating time is more than 7500 hr.
Except for slight leakage of lead during the first 100 hr of operation, there has been no further leakage.
The large lead pump seal with a 3 l/4-in.-dia rotating shaft has been operating for 3600 hr.
The average leakage rate for the time operated is $9\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{hr}$ .

The revised diagram shows that the composition $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (67.5-17.5-15 mole %; liquidus temperature, $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) has a higher $\mathrm{ThF_{4}}$ concentration than breeding mixtures of current interest, such as $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (71-16-13 mole %); also the liquidus temperature is slightly lower.
A description of the crystallization process for the composition $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (67.5-17.5-15 mole %) demonstrates this point.
On cooling to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two solid phases 3LiF·ThF<sub>4</sub> (composition located in triangular solid solution area) and 7LiF·6ThF<sub>4</sub> coprecipitate on a boundary curve.

As these two solids continue to freeze upon further cooling, the equilibrium liquid decreases in thorium concentration and leaves the boundary curve before reaching the peritectic point at $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (63-29.5-7.5 mole %, 452°C).
The liquid then changes composition toward another peritectic at $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (60.5-36.5-3 mole %) and freezes completely in the vicinity of the latter composition.

Since the quantity of liquid remaining in the region of the peritectic is much less than if no solid solution existed, most of the solids formed on cooling $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_{2} - ThF_{4}}$ (67.5-17.5-15 mole %) will be formed between 500 and $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and tendencies toward segregation will be reduced.
